speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Tishler. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Kishel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kishel. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kishel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kishel. OK. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows the synagogue in Kaluszyn. The houses next to it are all bombed out in 1939 when the Germans took this-- the town of Kaluszyn. The picture was taken in 1939 or in 1940 by a Polish young man who had it all the time. And I received it sometime-- maybe five, six years ago. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Later on, those houses were demolished to-- to rubble because they could not rebuild it, or the-- Some of them maybe was rebuilt, but most of them were demolished.

speaker: interviewer
text: who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The person that is in front of the picture, I don't remember who it is, because at the time this happened I wasn't there. I-- I cannot remember who the person is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the same synagogue that was shown before. The synagogue was destroyed-- or demolished-- at one time by un-- by-- with dynamite at the four corners and blown up in 1941 before the Germans invaded Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get this from the same person?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture I got from the same person that gave me the previous one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents our house in Kaluszyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one to the left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where the two men are standing from. But I do not know who they are. The size of the lot of the houses is about three times the width the house represents and about 100 yards deep. In the back of the yard, there were warehouses and other things. The picture was given to me by a-- by a man that visited Kaluszyn in 1975 or '80. This picture are my parents-- made in the 1920s. The picture was given to me by my aunt in Israel, who had them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents' names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents- my father's name was Moses. And my mother's name was Raisel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the occasion of this picture, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know the occasion. Maybe it's just a picture to send it to someone abroad or someone in the family for a purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kaluszyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my maternal great grandmother. I remember the lady-- in the 1930s. And she was about 105 years old. She died in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Miriam, I believe. Yes. Her name was Miriam, because they-- they have girls named after her. And they used to call [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] means [? Miriam for ?] [? Miriam. ?] But [INAUDIBLE] was a bakery. She had a bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture represents-- Wait. OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents my grandparents from my mother's side. My grandmother's name was Rivka. And my grandfather's name was Fischel. The picture was taken in the 1930s, because my grandfather died around approximately '35 or '36. 1935, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandmother was sent to Treblinka in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have these pictures through the same aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was given to me the same aunt that lived-- had emigrated to Israel in the 1930s. And she got all the pictures. And whatever pictures I show was mostly from-- through her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. OK. OK. This is a family picture of my mother's side [INAUDIBLE]. I am at the right side-- the first to the right. Next to me is a cousin called [? Dovit. ?] Below him, to the left, is a brother named [? Yosul. ?] The baby that sits on the lady's lap, her name is [? Hester. ?] Next to [? Hester ?] is my brother [? Avran. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then coming to the second row is my mother, Raizel. My sister, [? Heisura. ?] Then comes the lady that holds the baby. Her name is [? Chana. ?] She's a sister of my mother. Then comes the-- the lady-- [INAUDIBLE]. This is the lady-- my aunt who is in Israel, who gave me the picture. The next line in the middle is another sister of my mother. Her name is [? Hadasa. ?] She went during the war-- right after the Second World War, she ran away to Russia, not wanting to be with the Germans. And she died there. To the left of her on the last row is a cousin. Her name is Sarah. Sarah, [? a ?] cousin. And then next to her is also a cousin named Fischel. Just a minute. I got to remind myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And next to him is my aunt. Her name is-- she is the wife of the man that's standing. He is a brother of my mother. His name is [? Velvo. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Nahama?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Nahama is this one. And next to him is another-- is another man named [? Yosul, ?] also a brother of my-- of my mother. Next to him is a girl named [? Hava ?]. She's a sister of the man, the face, that his head is above [INAUDIBLE]. The one that le-- the one that did not survive in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The three you see here is myself to the right, a cousin [? Yosul, ?] who is the son of [? Chana, ?] and [? Dovet-- ?] we used to call him [? Dovcha-- ?] for the [INAUDIBLE] brother of [? Yosul. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents my aunt, Nahama, who is still in Israel, who gave me the pictures, and her brother, [INAUDIBLE], who was at that time in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken, and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Kaluszyn when he was on-- on furlough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it from Nahama, who is my aunt in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister. Her name is Chaya-- Chaya Soreh. The picture was made in 1937, '38. She died in Treblinka in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Kaluszyn. This is my brother and my sister-in-law. My brother survived the camps-- Majdanek, [INAUDIBLE] Buchenwald. And he came back home after the war, where I found him in 1945 when I came back from my camp. From camp. My brother died in August 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was [? Abraham ?] Kisielnicki. [INAUDIBLE] and in English [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife's name is Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in I believe in Germany in the late '40s. No, I'm sorry. Not in Germany. This picture was taken here in the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures are of myself. I am Meilech Kisielnicki, or Michael Kishel. The picture on the left was made about 1933 or 1934. And the picture on the right was made about a year or two later-- '35, '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for making those pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those pictures were taken because we used to make those pictures practically yearly or every two years to see the difference how we look. And it was a time that we got picture crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had them?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture had my-- my aunt in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is also of myself, Michael Kishel. At that time, I was still Meilech Kisielnicki. The picture on the left has been taken two weeks after I came home from the camp and lived in my hometown of Kaluszyn, eating and sleeping for almost two weeks when I gained 15 kilos. This is after gaining 15 kilos. The second picture was taken in 1948 when I was already in the United States. And this picture, I had preserved myself. On the left, the jacket that I wear was taken in a house-- in German house from-- which represents the jacket of [INAUDIBLE] [BACKGROUND NOISE] company-- a German company that worked for the supply to the German army. [INAUDIBLE] specialties. And probably also the shirt. But I'm sure the-- the jacket was taken there. And this is the jacket of the German [INAUDIBLE] organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my aunt in Israel. Her name is Nahama Hausmann. This is my aunt who had all the pictures of old ones, who preserved them and gave them to me to keep or to remind my family who and what they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at a Bat Mitzvah of my granddaughter, Emily, the one that shows in a white dress. The one sitting on the other side with a red dress is also my granddaughter, Aliyah. She lives in St. Louis. To the left of her-- standing-- is my grandson Ayle that lives in St. Louis. Next to him is I. Next to me is my daughter Meryl, who's the mother of Aliyah and Ayle. Next to her is my daughter Roslyn, who lives in Roslyn, Long Island. Next to her is her husband Marty. And next to Marty is my wife Simha. In front of Simha is my grandson Matthew, who's going to be bar mitzvah this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the-- at the pa-- at the Shalom Jewish Center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. In Roslyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Roslyn. I think Shalom. I forgot the name of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, Mr. Kishel, to please introduce your family to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my pleasure. Next to me is my lovely wife Simah. We are married for 42 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mike! Roslyn is 47 years--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 46. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got my figures--

speaker: interviewee
text: 47 years. And next to my wife is my lovely oldest daughter Roslyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And standing behind Roslyn is my son-in-law Marty. Marty Landsman. And next to her father is his daughter-- my granddaughter, the oldest granddaughter-- Emily. And next to Emily is my youngest grandson Matthew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for joining us now. We just went-- listened to a fairly lengthy tape from your father and your grandfather and your husband on his experiences in Poland and Germany during World War II, during the Shoah. And I was wondering if any of you would like to comment on how it impacted on your lives growing up with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, that would be me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be you. Or with your husband or with your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go first.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to formulate this. It's made me very much aware of my heritage. And sometimes when things are difficult for me, and I know what my father went through, what his family went through, what other Jews went through in Europe, I-- I put everything in perspective and say how-- how severe or how dire is this when other people have survived so much more? It's also installed a lot of guilt, where I have difficulty saying no to my father, even as an adult. That I'll do anything for him-- to the point of sometimes it's almost irrational-- because I love him and my mother dearly. And, um--

speaker: interviewer
text: But also looking at-- at other fathers and daughters or-- or children and parents, I don't see how that would really differ. I belong to a group of children of survivors. And we had meetings-- monthly meetings. And the first time that we met, we all sat around and said, how did this impact you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I was the first one to go. And I said, you know, sometimes I feel such guilt, and I'm sure that I'm the only one who feels this. And any or I'd say most of my feelings, most of my impressions that I expressed, were expressed by 95% of the people around-- the other children of survivors. Sometimes depression, sometimes pride, all those different things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came from Costa Rica.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland. My father left Poland in 1929. We came to Costa Rica. We left Poland in '35, and we came to Costa Rica in '36. We left in December. It took us a month to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: My daughter talks about guilt. I feel guilt. And I ask myself everyday, what have I done in this world to have escaped all of that? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was God's will.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at the same time, my husband was a good husband, a wonderful father, a very good provider. And we lived very nicely, not any different from any of my American friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I always say that God has been very good to us. He's given us good health, lovely children, grandchildren, a few nice, good friends. What else is there? And we have been basically very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Emily?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to take a crack?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, I've studied-- in many of my courses in school, I studied World War II and the Holocaust. And it's always-- I feel that it's always a different-- it's always different for me than for my classmates because I have a personal connection. And, um-- Once, in one of my Hebrew school classes, my grandfather came in and he showed pictures of his town. And he talked about his life in the town before the war and during the war. And some kids got a lot out of it, because [BACKGROUND NOISE] they had-- their relatives weren't in the war, things like that. So-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want me to answer that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Let it ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let it ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? Let it ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And this summer, I just went to Israel. I just came back from Israel. And it was-- it was very special for me because it was going to the Holy Land and going to the Promised Land and everything like that. And it was-- it was-- really-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not telling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I guess that it's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Matthew? You're going to be Bar Mitzvah, I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yup. I don't really know what to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: How was it to live with us-- having been born to our family-- with your grandfather, with me, with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: How was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we won't test you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about Marty? Why don't you say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not going to leave you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm kind of at a loss for words.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are, obviously, an integral part of this family. I'm sure it's had some impact on you too.

speaker: interviewee
text: The experiences that Mike has had, I think, have made my-- my family unique, even though it may have been for tragic reasons. But we are different. And we have a different sense of-- of history because we really were living through-- through history. And every year that we go to the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising commemorations, it takes a piece out of you. And from that point of view, you know, we are special. And we talk about that all the time-- that our family is going to have to carry forward those-- those thoughts and remember what's made us so unique. And it's something that we can't ever forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Kishel, thank you so much for sharing your story and your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for listening to me and taking my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that he enjoyed every minute of it.
